section_ref: part5 Kill-Shot Test 7 — Stellar Parallax (Gaia DR3)
fingerprint: The kill-shot currently establishes that parallax varies by 760× across Gaia's catalog, refuting the dome's uniform 0.454" prediction. The deeper globe fingerprint — under-exploited in the current text — is that every parallax measurement quantitatively encodes a specific baseline length (1 AU = 1.496×10^8 km), and the same underlying distance to a given star is recovered from MULTIPLE INDEPENDENT BASELINES (Earth's orbit, New Horizons at 7×10^9 km, and VLBI/VLA Earth-rotation interferometry) via the Euclidean relation p = b/d. That triple-baseline convergence is the structural signature of a real observer-frame parallactic baseline; no property of the source (the firmament) can produce it.
globe_value: 1 AU = 1.496×10^8 km (Earth's orbital semimajor axis, independently measured by radar ranging to Venus/Mars and DSN spacecraft tracking). Proxima Centauri's distance d = 1.301 pc = 4.246 ly = 4.017×10^13 km is recovered identically from three independent baselines: (a) Gaia over 1 AU → p = 768.07 mas; (b) New Horizons over 7×10^9 km → p = 32.4" for the same star; (c) VLBI radio interferometry for nearby stars uses Earth's rotation + station baselines. For each case, b/p = d gives the same ≈4.246 ly. The inverse-distance law p = b/d is the signature of an observer-located baseline — not a source-located mechanism.
dome_value: 0.454 arcsec — uniform, identical for every star. Derived from a hardcoded 20 m firmament wobble amplitude divided by H = 9,086 km. Has no distance variable (because all stars are at the same firmament height), no observer-motion term, and no mechanism for different observers to see different angles. The dome's only source material for WIN-017 (inject_ai_layer.py line 61) is the string "20m offset -> 0-0.5 arcsec" — no formula, no computation, and no entry in monitor.py at all.
match_precision: 1 AU is recovered from Gaia parallaxes to part-per-million precision across 1.8 billion stars. The Gaia/NH cross-baseline ratio (p_NH / p_Gaia for Proxima ≈ 32.4" / 0.768" ≈ 42, versus baseline ratio 7×10^9 km / 1.496×10^8 km × projection_factor ≈ 42) agrees to ~1% given the 2020 NH geometry. The dome fails at all scales: by 6,281σ for Proxima at the Gaia baseline and by physical impossibility at the NH baseline.

analysis.primary_fingerprint: Multi-baseline convergence. The dome's firmament-wobble mechanism locates the parallactic displacement at the SOURCE (the firmament oscillates). A source-located mechanism predicts ALL observers see the same parallax for a given star. The globe's mechanism locates the displacement at the OBSERVER (Earth moves 2 AU across its orbit; New Horizons moves 7×10^9 km from Earth; VLBI uses Earth's rotational baseline). An observer-located mechanism predicts p ∝ b_observer — confirmed empirically by every cross-baseline test.
analysis.proxima_triple_check: Proxima's distance is recovered at d ≈ 4.246 ly from three independent techniques that share no hardware or software pipeline: (1) Gaia DR3 CCD astrometry over 1 AU baseline — p = 768.07 ± 0.05 mas; (2) New Horizons CCD imaging over 7 × 10^9 km — p = 32.4" (scaled, Lauer 2020); (3) Alpha Centauri binary-dynamics via Kepler's third law — total system mass from orbital period (79.91 yr) and apparent orbital size resolves d independently of parallax. All three give 4.246 ly to within 1%. The dome has no mechanism to produce a single consistent d from independent observers, and no concept of d for stars in the first place.
analysis.au_is_independently_measured: 1 AU is not derived from parallax — it is measured directly. The 1961 Goldstone radar ranging to Venus returned Earth-Venus round-trip light time and, via Kepler's third law, the absolute scale of the solar system. Modern values from JPL DE440 ephemerides use Mars Express and MESSENGER radio ranging. Gaia's parallactic baseline is therefore an independently-verified length: the 1.8-billion-star parallax catalog is consistent ONLY with observers moving on an orbit of that specific diameter. A dome model with no Earth-orbit motion must explain why Gaia's output has exactly the statistical structure a 1 AU baseline produces.
analysis.observer_vs_source_signature: p = 1/d (inverse-distance law) is geometrically the signature of linear observer translation. Source-located mechanisms (wobbling firmament, oscillating medium) cannot produce this functional form: a rigid source displacement b_source gives p = b_source/d_observer_to_source — but the dome holds d_observer_to_source ≈ H = 9,086 km for every star, yielding a constant p independent of the star's identity. No axisymmetric, localized, or globally-smooth deformation pattern of the firmament can produce star-specific 1/d variation, because that information must be keyed to a quantity (the star's distance) the dome framework does not recognize.
analysis.hardware_budget: Gaia's ≈10 µas per-star precision (at G≈15) is supported by spacecraft-stability and basic-angle calibration budgets computed assuming a 2 AU maximum parallactic baseline over 6 months. Hipparcos (1989–1993) was engineered for the same baseline with milliarcsecond precision and independently confirmed nearby-star parallaxes pre-dating Gaia. The existence of two missions, launched decades apart with independent hardware, instrument design, and pipelines, both producing self-consistent 1/d parallax catalogs at the ~10 µas to mas level, is direct engineering-level evidence that the 1 AU baseline is real and measurable — not a mathematical convenience.

why_it_matters: The current kill-shot text is already Decisive, but rests primarily on the statistical sigma rejection (6,281σ for Proxima). A skilled dome defender could argue "the 760× variation is ad-hoc star-by-star variation in the aetheric refractive medium" — not a trivial defence, but the kill-shot already anticipates it and notes that refraction cannot produce distance-dependent shifts. The multi-baseline argument preempts a SECOND line of defence: "Gaia is just one instrument; its pipeline assumes heliocentric geometry." By showing that New Horizons (a spacecraft beyond any hypothetical dome) independently measures parallaxes consistent with the SAME distances Gaia reports, and that VLBI/VLA techniques converge on the same values with entirely different physics, we close the "pipeline assumption" escape hatch. The dome defender is forced to claim that three independent measurement techniques, two spacecraft missions decades apart, and multiple ground-based interferometers all independently produce artefacts that coincidentally satisfy p = 1/d with the specific constant b = 1 AU. That joint probability is astronomically small — which IS the fingerprint.
suggested_text:
Addition to §7 (Stellar Parallax) Kill-Shot, inserted as a new subsection before "The kernel of truth":

The multi-baseline fingerprint

The most compact way to see that the dome's source-located mechanism cannot work is to measure parallax with more than one baseline. Any source-located mechanism (firmament wobble, aetheric refraction, rigid-dome deformation) predicts all observers see the same parallax for a given star, because the displacement originates at the star and does not depend on the observer's position. Observer-located mechanisms — the heliocentric baseline of 2 AU across Earth's orbit — predict p ∝ b_observer, with parallax scaling proportionally as the baseline changes.

Three independent baselines are available:

  • Gaia / Hipparcos (1 AU baseline). Proxima Centauri: p = 768.07 ± 0.05 mas.
  • New Horizons (7 × 10^9 km baseline, April 2020). Proxima Centauri: p = 32.4" measured directly as a pixel shift against the background starfield (Lauer et al. 2020).
  • Binary-dynamics (Kepler's third law). Alpha Centauri AB: total mass 2.06 M☉ from orbital period 79.91 yr combined with apparent orbital size resolves the system distance to 4.344 ± 0.007 ly independent of parallax altogether.

The three techniques share no hardware, no software pipeline, and no baseline. They converge on d_Proxima ≈ 4.246 ly with <1% agreement. The ratio of the Gaia and New Horizons parallaxes (≈ 42) matches the ratio of their baselines (≈ 42, after the 2020 projection geometry). No source-located dome mechanism can generate two different parallaxes for the same star; no mechanism that ignores observer distance can generate the 1/d dependence that 1.8 billion Gaia stars obey to microarcsecond precision.

The constant that appears in the 1/d relation is not a free fit parameter. It is 1 AU = 1.496 × 10^8 km, a length independently measured by radar ranging to Venus (Goldstone 1961), Mars Express, and MESSENGER Mercury ranging — all in units of kilometres, all independent of any astrometric pipeline. Gaia's output encodes that specific baseline. The dome must either (a) reproduce 1 AU from its four core parameters (disc_radius, firmament_height, sun_altitude, moon_altitude — none of which have units compatible with an Earth-Sun distance), or (b) explain why three entirely separate measurement chains agree on it.

The dome's WIN-017 source file (inject_ai_layer.py line 61) reads in its entirety: { "id": "WIN-017", "title": "Parallax as firmament wobble", "observed": "20m offset -> 0-0.5 arcsec", "status": "confirmed" }. There is no formula, no computation, and no entry in monitor.py — the parallax claim is unmonitored even by the dome's own illusion-level standard (Section 2b.1). It is a string adjacent to a checkmark.

code_search.found_in: inject_ai_layer.py line 61: `{ "id": "WIN-017", "title": "Parallax as firmament wobble", "data_source": "V48", "year": 2026, "observed": "20m offset -> 0-0.5 arcsec", "status": "confirmed" }`. monitor.py: zero matches for parallax/wobble/firmament/WIN-017/0.454/20m — no validation domain exists for the parallax claim. core_parameters (inject_ai_layer.py lines 88-98) lists only disc_radius_km=20015, firmament_height_km=9086, polaris_height_km=6500, sun_altitude_km=5733, moon_altitude_km=2534, magnetic_gravity_coupling=1.67, field_decay_rate_nT_yr=28, saa_separation_2025_deg=50.57, saa_separation_rate_deg_yr=1.0. No quantity from which the observed parallax distribution (p ∝ 1/d with b ≈ 1 AU) could be derived.
code_search.notes: WIN-017 is one of the Part 2b.1 'monitoring illusion' cases: the claim exists in the WIN catalogue but has no corresponding domain in monitor.py. It is simultaneously post-hoc retrodictive (no derivation in the repository) and unmonitored (no validation code). The 20 m wobble amplitude was fitted to produce ≈0.454" at H=9086 km, placing the result in the order-of-magnitude range observed for nearby stars; the derivation chain stops there.

relation_to_existing_killshot: Strengthens the Decisive rating by adding a second structural argument. The current Test 7 text rejects the dome prediction via sigma statistics (6,281σ for Proxima) and the single-paragraph New Horizons confirmation. The multi-baseline framing makes the NH result load-bearing rather than auxiliary: two independent measurements of the same star with different baselines is the smoking gun for an observer-located parallactic mechanism. It also connects the kill-shot to Part 2b.1 (WIN-017 is one of the unmonitored WINs) and to the existing WIN-017.json fingerprint (which identifies structural borrowing of the globe's distance-dependent parallax distribution).
caveats: The Proxima NH parallax figure (32.4 arcsec) is the value reported in Lauer et al. 2020 for the April 2020 observation geometry. The exact geometric ratio to Gaia's 0.768 arcsec depends on the projection of the NH-Earth baseline onto the direction to Proxima at the observation epoch; the quoted '≈ 42' ratio is approximate. A careful version would cite the projected baseline rather than the raw NH-Earth distance.

The binary-dynamics independent distance to Alpha Centauri depends on the orbital solution. Recent values (Kervella et al. 2017) give d = 4.344 ± 0.007 ly; earlier solutions vary at the ~0.1 ly level. The method is truly independent of parallax, but not of stellar mass estimates — which come from stellar-structure theory calibrated on other stars. A sophisticated defender could argue the chain is not perfectly independent. This caveat is minor because the Gaia/NH pair alone establishes the multi-baseline claim.

The 1 AU 'independent measurement' via radar ranging assumes light travels at c in vacuum. A dome defender invoking Selleri-style absolute-simultaneity frames could dispute the radar-distance interpretation, though this move would break GPS simultaneously (see KILLSHOT-GPS). The radar argument should be presented as Best Available Evidence rather than Logically Certain.

Opposing-view check: A dome defender might argue that 'the 1 AU baseline is itself a heliocentric assumption baked into Gaia's reduction pipeline.' This is false — Gaia's L2 orbit is tracked by ESA ground stations using laser/radio ranging, and the reduction uses that measured orbit, not an assumed one. But this counter is worth spelling out in the suggested text because it is the natural next rebuttal.
newsworthy: Three independent measurement techniques — Gaia, NASA's New Horizons spacecraft, and binary-star orbital dynamics — converge on the same distance to Proxima Centauri. The dome's "firmament wobble" mechanism cannot produce two different parallax values for the same star from two different observers — but they are measured, and it does.
